bibliography.title: Photophysiological stress in scleractinian corals in to short-term sedimentation response

study.study_location: Magnetic Island, turbid nearshore fringing reef, Great Barrier Reef (GBR), Australia

exposure.sediment_composition: Fine, muddy sediment collected from 3 m depth behind the breakwater wall of the AIMS jetty; sieved through a 3-mm mesh; aerated for two days; sediment contained 2.37% carbon, 0.13% nitrogen, and 459 Ag/g phosphorous. Sediment remained on fragments during exposure.
exposure.notes: Laboratory Expt 1.1 used mean sediment load 151 ± 37 mg DW cm−2 (range 79–234 mg cm−2) (text: "It averaged 151 F 37 mg DW cm[�][2] (range: 79–234 mg cm[�][2] )."). Exposure durations: 0, 12, 18, 24, 36 h. Fv/Fm measured after sediment removal and dark adaptation; pigment and zooxanthellae measured on frozen subsamples.

responses.level: below 0.1 in most colonies after 36 h coverage
responses.response_source: Abstract; Results text for Expt 1.1: "maximum yields of sediment-covered fragments declined steadily and reached levels below 0.1 in most colonies after 36 h coverage". Also Fig. 3 legend: "Fv/Fm (backtransformed) ... (N = 4–11 colony fragments per treatment, 15 measurements per fragment)."

Section 3.1.1.1 (Laboratory Expt 1.1): "fell to 1.5–1.9 � 10[6] cells cm[�][2] after 36 h sediment exposure"; Fig. 3 legend: "Zooxanthellae density (N = 4–7 fragments per treatment, two subsamples with eight zooxanthellae counts each per subsample and fragment)."

Section 3.1.1.1 (Laboratory Expt 1.1): "sediment for 36 h contained 1.3–2.5 Ag cm[�][2]"; Fig. 3 legend: "Chlorophyll concentration per unit surface area (N = 4–7 fragments per treatment, three replicates per fragment)."

study.study_location: Magnetic Island, turbid nearshore fringing reef, Great Barrier Reef (GBR), Australia

exposure.sediment_composition: Fine, muddy sediment as described for laboratory; applied as distributed evenly in water column until sediment settled after 6 h; sediment remained on fragments during exposure.
exposure.notes: Laboratory recovery assessment (Experiment 1.2): fragments exposed to 0, 12, 18, and 24 h sediment, then recovery monitored up to 7 days by repeated Fv/Fm measurements. Mean sediment load used in Expt 1.1 is stated; Expt 1.2 is described without reporting dose mean.

responses.level: recovered to normal Fv/Fm ratios within 24–36 h (after 12 and 18 h exposure); after 24 h exposure, two fragments loaded with 109 and 138 mg cm−2 showed further declines after wash-off and died; one fragment loaded with 107 mg cm−2 recovered within 48 h
responses.response_source: Section 3.1.1.2: "All fragments ... exposed to sediments for 12 and 18 h, recovered to normal Fv/Fm ratios within 24–36 h... After 24 h of exposure, one coral fragment loaded with 107 mg cm[�][2] sediment fully recovered to initial values within 48 h. Two other fragments, loaded for 24 h with 109 and 138 mg cm[�][2] ... showed further declines ... and died".
n/r (Zooxanthellae density outcomes for Expt 1.2 not reported in supplied text).
n/r (Chlorophyll outcomes for Expt 1.2 not reported in supplied text).

study.study_location: Magnetic Island, turbid nearshore fringing reef, Great Barrier Reef (GBR), Australia

exposure.sediment_composition: Fine, muddy sediment (laboratory description) applied to about 50% of colony surface (patch), leaving remaining surface clean; later repeated in field with fresh sediment exposure of 22 h.
exposure.notes: Laboratory Experiment 1.3: about 50% surface covered for 48 h; Expt 1.3 later repeated in field on four colonies each of Echinopora lamellosa and massive Porites, exposed to sediments for 22 h. For M. peltiformis, response categories are tissues: C0 (before), C end (control free of sediment), E end (edge), S end (under sediment). Table 3 provides mean differences vs Cend; no absolute DF/F mV values reported.

study.study_location: Coral Bay, Ningaloo Reef Tract, Western Australia (23 ° 09 ′ S; 113 ° 45 ′ E)

exposure.sediment_composition: Terrestrial sediment bricks; no less than 90% silt/clay (without cement stabiliser) delivered by softened rammed earth bricks
exposure.notes: Sedimentation rates at treatment sites (Table 1 overall). Reference sites are paired but this setup represents sediment treatment. Values reported as mg.cm2.day-1 ±95% ci (Table 1).

responses.response_source: Abstract: "Settlement was significantly lower in the sediment treatments where it reached only 71% of levels in reference areas." Evidence also aligns with Fig/Table statement: "Settlement in the sediment treatment areas was 71% of that in the reference areas (Table 2, χ[2 ] = 5.32, p=0.021)."

Abstract/Results: "number of juvenile corals surviving at sediment treated sites after 8 months was 39% of levels at reference sites" (Abstract) and "by this time they were 39% of the number surviving in the reference sites" (Results). Mortality fraction relative to reference survival is therefore 1-0.39 = 0.61; no direct mortality proportion is explicitly reported.

study.study_location: Coral Bay, Ningaloo Reef Tract, Western Australia (23 ° 09 ′ S; 113 ° 45 ′ E)

responses.response_source: Abstract/Results define reference as the 100% level for comparison: "Settlement was significantly lower in the sediment treatments where it reached only 71% of levels in reference areas." responses.unit: fraction (1 - survival fraction) relative to reference
responses.response_source: Abstract/Results: survival in sediment treatments is 39% of reference after 8 months. By definition, reference survival is 100% relative to itself; this implies 0 relative mortality. Absolute mortality/survival fractions are not reported for reference in the text.
